title: What a foreigner learns about queuing in Hanoi
summary: A Norwegian engineer arrives in Hanoi convinced the queues are chaos. After a year, he understands he was reading the wrong system.
body:
When I moved to Hanoi for a two-year contract with a small engineering firm, the first thing that frustrated me was queueing. At the bank, at the train station, at the noodle stall around the corner from my flat in Ba Dinh, the queue seemed to me chaotic. People stood close to each other. They handed money over heads. They shouted orders past me. I was Norwegian; I had lived in Oslo, in Trondheim, in Bergen. I had stood in a Norwegian queue. I knew, I thought, what a queue was.

For the first three months I complained, mostly to other foreigners, and I behaved badly. I would stand exactly behind the person in front of me with about forty centimetres between us, as if drawing a line, and look at people who slipped past me with what I imagined to be a quiet, dignified disapproval. They probably saw a tall man making an unhappy face. They were not wrong.

What I noticed, slowly, was that the queues moved. People at the noodle stall were served in an order I could not see, but the order existed. The woman who had been waiting longest was served first. Money handed across heads was, more often than not, an older customer paying for a younger person who had come in after them. The cook remembered who had arrived when. She kept a list in her head of perhaps fifteen people. She made no mistakes in the four months I watched her.

The queue, in other words, was not an absence of order. It was a different order. It was held in the collective memory of the people present, not enforced by a line on the floor. My Norwegian queue was held by the floor. Theirs was held by the cook, by the regulars, and by mutual attention. When I finally understood this, I felt embarrassed. I had been reading the system in the language of my country and concluding it was broken because it did not match my grammar.

There were, however, costs. A new arrival, somebody from out of town who did not know the cook and whom the cook did not know, could be quietly skipped. The collective memory worked beautifully for those inside it, less well for strangers. The Norwegian queue, with its harder, more rigid rule, was clumsier for the regular, but kinder to the stranger. Neither system was simply better.

In my last week before flying home, I went to the noodle stall to say goodbye to the cook. She gave me my bowl, refused my money, and said something I had heard her say to other regulars: 'Today is on me.' I had not been there longest that morning, and there were two newer customers behind me who had been waiting. They smiled and waved me through. I had become, after fourteen months, a person the cook remembered. The collective memory had quietly let me in.

I have lived back in Oslo for three years now. Our queues are still the queues I grew up in. I respect them. I do not, however, think of them as the only correct shape any longer. I think of them as one of several. The cook in Ba Dinh, who probably did not know she was teaching me anything, taught me more about reading other people's systems than my engineering degree.

annotations.meaningVi: ngữ pháp (ở đây: 'cú pháp' của hệ thống)
ra hiệu cho tôi đi trước
công ty kỹ thuật / kỹ sư
người từ nơi khác đến / không phải người địa phương

grammarNotes.title: Used to and past habit forms
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng 'used to + V' và 'would + V' để kể thói quen quá khứ. Trong bài: 'I would stand exactly behind the person in front of me' ('would' chỉ thói quen lặp lại trong quá khứ). 'I had stood in a Norwegian queue. I knew, I thought, what a queue was.' (past perfect + past simple — trải nghiệm trước, nhận thức sau). 'I had been reading the system in the language of my country' (past perfect continuous — kéo dài trong quá khứ trước một thời điểm khác).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses 'used to + V' and 'would + V' for past habits. In the passage: 'I would stand exactly behind the person in front of me' ('would' marks a repeated past habit). 'I had stood in a Norwegian queue. I knew, I thought, what a queue was.' (past perfect + past simple — earlier experience, later judgement). 'I had been reading the system in the language of my country' (past perfect continuous — extended past activity before another past moment).

translationVi:
Khi tôi chuyển đến Hà Nội theo một hợp đồng hai năm với một công ty kỹ thuật nhỏ, điều đầu tiên làm tôi bực là việc xếp hàng. Ở ngân hàng, ga tàu, quầy phở góc phố nhà tôi ở Ba Đình, hàng người trông với tôi lộn xộn. Người ta đứng sát nhau. Đưa tiền qua đầu nhau. Hét gọi món qua tôi. Tôi là người Na Uy; tôi đã sống ở Oslo, Trondheim, Bergen. Tôi đã đứng trong hàng kiểu Na Uy. Tôi, tôi nghĩ, biết hàng là gì.

Ba tháng đầu tôi than phiền, chủ yếu với người nước ngoài khác, và tôi cư xử tệ. Tôi đứng đúng sau người trước tôi, cách khoảng bốn mươi cm, như đang vẽ một đường, và nhìn người lách qua bằng cái mà tôi tưởng là sự không tán thành lặng lẽ, có phẩm giá. Có lẽ họ thấy một anh cao, mặt nhăn. Họ không sai.

Dần dần tôi nhận thấy: hàng vẫn chạy. Người ở quầy phở được phục vụ theo một thứ tự tôi không thấy, nhưng thứ tự đó tồn tại. Người phụ nữ đợi lâu nhất được phục vụ trước. Tiền đưa qua đầu, hầu hết là người lớn tuổi trả tiền cho người trẻ tới sau. Cô bán phở nhớ ai tới khi nào. Cô có một danh sách trong đầu, có lẽ mười lăm người. Trong bốn tháng tôi quan sát, cô không sai lần nào.

Nói cách khác, hàng không phải là sự thiếu trật tự. Đó là một trật tự khác. Nó được giữ trong trí nhớ tập thể của những người có mặt, không phải bằng vạch trên sàn. Hàng kiểu Na Uy của tôi được giữ bởi sàn. Hàng của họ được giữ bởi cô bán, bởi khách quen, và bởi sự chú ý lẫn nhau. Khi cuối cùng tôi hiểu, tôi thấy ngượng. Tôi đã đọc hệ thống bằng ngôn ngữ của nước tôi và kết luận nó hỏng vì nó không khớp ngữ pháp của tôi.

Tuy nhiên có cái giá. Một người mới tới, người ngoài tỉnh không quen cô bán và cô bán không quen, có thể bị bỏ qua một cách lặng lẽ. Trí nhớ tập thể tuyệt vời với người ở trong nó, kém với người lạ. Hàng kiểu Na Uy, với quy tắc cứng hơn, vụng với khách quen, nhưng tử tế hơn với người lạ. Không có hệ thống nào đơn giản là tốt hơn.

Tuần cuối trước khi về nước, tôi tới quầy chào tạm biệt cô. Cô đưa tôi tô phở, từ chối nhận tiền, và nói câu tôi từng nghe cô nói với khách quen: 'Hôm nay tôi mời.' Sáng đó tôi không phải người đợi lâu nhất, và có hai khách mới hơn đứng sau đợi. Họ cười và ra hiệu cho tôi đi trước. Sau mười bốn tháng, tôi đã trở thành người cô bán nhớ. Trí nhớ tập thể lặng lẽ cho tôi vào.

Tôi đã trở về Oslo ba năm. Hàng ở đây vẫn là hàng tôi lớn lên cùng. Tôi tôn trọng chúng. Tuy nhiên, tôi không còn nghĩ chúng là hình dáng đúng duy nhất. Tôi nghĩ chúng là một trong vài kiểu. Cô bán phở ở Ba Đình, có lẽ không biết mình đang dạy tôi điều gì, dạy tôi nhiều hơn về cách đọc hệ thống của người khác hơn cả tấm bằng kỹ sư.

questions.prompt: Where did the narrator move, and from where?
options: From Norway (Oslo, Trondheim, Bergen) to Hanoi for a 2-year engineering contract; living in Ba Dinh

questions.prompt: What was his first impression of queuing?
options: Chaotic — people close, money over heads, shouted orders past him

questions.prompt: How did he behave for the first three months?
options: Stood ~40cm behind people, looked at line-jumpers with 'quiet disapproval'; complained to other foreigners

questions.prompt: What did he gradually realise the cook was doing?
options: Holding a ~15-person mental list of who arrived when, in order — and making no mistakes in 4 months

questions.prompt: How does he reframe the difference in the fourth paragraph?
options: Not absence of order, but a different order — held by collective memory, not a line on the floor

questions.prompt: What did he feel when he understood?
options: Embarrassed — he had read the system in his country's language and called it broken

questions.prompt: What cost did the Hanoi system have?
options: Strangers/new arrivals could be quietly skipped; the system was great for regulars, weaker for outsiders

questions.prompt: What did the Norwegian queue offer in comparison?
options: A harder, more rigid rule — clumsier for regulars but kinder to strangers

questions.prompt: What happens in his last week at the stall?
options: Cook refuses payment ('Today is on me'); two newer customers wave him through; he is recognised as a regular

questions.prompt: What does he conclude about Norwegian queues now?
options: He respects them, but no longer thinks of them as the only correct shape — one of several
He thinks they are inferior to Hanoi's

questions.prompt: What does he say about the cook?
options: She probably didn't know she was teaching him — but taught him more about reading other people's systems than his engineering degree
She was a teacher by training
What looks like chaos to a foreigner is sometimes a different, working order held by mutual attention rather than physical rules — and learning to read it changes how you judge your own systems.
Foreigners should not move to Vietnam.

When I moved to Hanoi for a two-year contract with a small engineering firm, the first thing that frustrated me was {{b1}}. At the bank, at the train station, at the noodle stall around the corner from my flat in Ba Dinh, the queue seemed to me {{b2}}. People stood close to each other. They handed money over heads. They shouted orders past me. I was Norwegian; I had lived in Oslo, in Trondheim, in Bergen. I had stood in a Norwegian queue. I knew, I thought, what a queue was.

They probably saw a tall man making an unhappy face. They were not wrong.

What I noticed, slowly, was that the queues moved. People at the noodle stall were served in an order I could not see, but the order existed. The woman who had been waiting longest was served first. Money handed across heads was, more often than not, an older customer paying for a younger person who had come in after them. The cook remembered who had arrived when. She kept a {{b4}} in her head of perhaps fifteen people. She made no mistakes in the four months I watched her.

The queue, in other words, was not an absence of order. It was a different order. It was held in the {{b5}} memory of the people present, not enforced by a line on the floor. My Norwegian queue was held by the floor. Theirs was held by the cook, by the regulars, and by mutual attention. When I finally understood this, I felt {{b6}}. I had been reading the system in the language of my country and concluding it was broken because it did not match my grammar.

There were, however, costs. A new arrival, somebody from out of town who did not know the cook and whom the cook did not know, could be quietly {{b7}}. The collective memory worked beautifully for those inside it, less well for strangers. The Norwegian queue, with its harder, more rigid rule, was clumsier for the regular, but {{b8}} to the stranger. In my last week before flying home, I went to the noodle stall to say goodbye to the cook. I had not been there longest that morning, and there were two newer customers behind me who had been waiting. They smiled and waved me through. I have lived back in Oslo for three years now. Our queues are still the queues I grew up in. I respect them. I do not, however, think of them as the only {{b10}} shape any longer. I think of them as one of several. The cook in Ba Dinh, who probably did not know she was teaching me anything, taught me more about reading other people's {{b11}} than my engineering degree.

blanks.explanation: Kept a list in her head.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says he was 'reading the system in the language of my country.' Think of a habit in your own culture that visitors often misread as disorder or rudeness. What is the 'collective memory' or unwritten rule that they are missing — and what does it cost newcomers?
